narrative:
El factor dominante en el mercado es la frágil tregua de dos semanas entre Estados Unidos e Irán, que condiciona la percepción de riesgo global. Esta pausa temporal en el conflicto, con una reunión cara a cara programada en Pakistán para el 10 y 11 de abril de 2026, genera expectativas de estabilidad, pero la continuidad de bombardeos israelíes en Líbano y la intención iraní de cobrar peaje por el paso en el estrecho de Ormuz mantienen la tensión geopolítica. Esta dinámica sostiene los precios del petróleo por encima de los 100 dólares el barril y alimenta preocupaciones inflacionarias, elementos clave que impactan en la valoración de activos y en la política monetaria.

El mercado responde con cautela ante este escenario. Los futuros de acciones en Estados Unidos muestran caídas moderadas, reflejando incertidumbre sobre la durabilidad de la tregua, mientras que los rendimientos de los bonos del Tesoro se mantienen estables, evidenciando una demanda por activos seguros sin un movimiento claro hacia una relajación monetaria. La persistencia de precios elevados en el crudo y la amenaza latente de nuevas hostilidades limitan el apetito por riesgo y mantienen la volatilidad en niveles elevados, condicionando la toma de decisiones de los inversores.

Desde una perspectiva institucional, se observa un optimismo contenido respecto a que la tregua se mantenga durante las próximas semanas, lo que podría favorecer un repunte moderado en las acciones. Sin embargo, la desconfianza persiste debido a las violaciones en Líbano y el control iraní sobre Hormuz, que sostienen un riesgo estructural en la oferta energética y, por ende, una prima inflacionaria elevada. Esto desafía las expectativas de un posible alivio en la política monetaria por parte de la Reserva Federal. El capital institucional probablemente se posiciona con prudencia, equilibrando exposición a renta variable con cobertura ante la inflación y volatilidad geopolítica, mientras monitorea de cerca los datos macroeconómicos y los desarrollos en la región.

Los eventos clave que pueden validar o modificar esta narrativa incluyen la reunión entre Estados Unidos e Irán en Pakistán, que será determinante para confirmar la vigencia de la tregua, y la publicación de datos inflacionarios estadounidenses, como el IPC y el PCE, que influirán en las decisiones de la Fed. Asimismo, la evolución de la actividad militar israelí en Líbano y las acciones iraníes en el estrecho de Ormuz serán indicadores críticos para evaluar riesgos de escalada. La publicación del Beige Book de la Fed también aportará señales sobre la percepción económica y la posible orientación de la política monetaria en un contexto de incertidumbre geopolítica persistente.
regime:
🏷️ MARKET REGIME: Régimen Transicional / Volátil 🟡

El mercado muestra rebotes comprados con cautela, pero la continuidad de rupturas es inconsistente, generando movimientos erráticos y volatilidad elevada. Típicamente, en situaciones como esta, la reacción a noticias es mixta y el sentimiento oscila entre optimismo limitado y preocupación latente. La falta de un impulso claro y la persistencia de riesgos subyacentes mantienen la incertidumbre, dificultando establecer tendencias sostenidas. Traders novatos deben priorizar gestión activa del riesgo y evitar posiciones direccionales fuertes.

vital.source_subject: Vital Knowledge: Vital Dawn for Thursday April 9, 2026
vital.market_levels:
Market levels – the US equity futures are in the red so far this morning: S&P -30 points/43bp, Dow -196 points/41bp, Nasdaq -104 points/42bp, and R2 K -19.8 points/75bp. The major Eurozone indices are down 70-90bp, with underperformance in autos, industrials, luxury, tech, and media while utilities, energy, insurance, and chemicals outperform . Stocks in Asia mostly saw losses on Thursday (with some exceptions): TPX -0.9%, NKY -0.73%, Hang Seng -0.54%, HSCEI -0.75%, SHCOMP -0.72%, Shenzhen -0.61%, Taiwan TAIEX +0.29%, Korea KOSPI -1.61%, Australia ASX 200 +0.24%, and India is down ~1.1-1.3%. Treasuries are flat across the curve, and Fed expectations aren’t moving much either (the market is pricing in 7bp of rate cuts this year). The DXY is ticking down 10bp. Brent is rallying 340bp to ~$98 (the recent low was ~$90.40 from Wed morning). Gold is rallying 30bp while silver trades flat. Bitcoin is about unchanged at ~$71.3 K.
•
vital.whats_happening:
What’s happening this morning – equities are giving back a bit of the Wed rally as investors sit on the edge of their seats to see whether the fragile ceasefire holds . Incremental news since the US close yesterday has been pretty quiet , and the next major (scheduled) event will be the (tentative) US-Iran meeting set to take place Sat morning in Pakistan (late on Friday in the US). While there were ceasefire violations earlier Wed, it seems like the détente is very much in place so far Thurs – there were no reports of strikes on Iran, and Iran seems to have fired off no drones/missiles of its own , while the foreign ministers of Iran and Saudi Arabia spoke this morning for the first time since the war commenced . The two big flies in the ceasefire ointment are Lebanon (Israel apparently promised Washington it would dial back the intensity of its strikes against Hezbollah for the next two weeks, but this theater is not included in the ceasefire) and Hormuz (where traffic remains depressed and Iran seems to be pushing forward with plans for a toll). In AI , the big news isn’t Meta’s Muse Spark (which we wrote about here on Wed) but instead the new models from Anthropic and Open AI . Anthropic’s warnings about its new model being so powerful that it couldn’t be released right away to the public weighed heavily on software sentiment , and it sounds like Open AI will be adopting a similar approach to its latest model (according to Axios ).
•
vital.thinking_about_markets:
How we’re thinking about markets – there is a lot of skepticism and cynicism when it comes to the cease fire and this, coupled with continued technical imbalances (in terms of lean long positioning and negative sentiment) point to more equity upside in the immediate term (we think the ceasefire will hold , for reasons outlined in a piece published Wed morning; the next major event will probably be the face-to-face talks between US and Iranian officials tentatively scheduled for Friday in Pakistan ). In addition, consumer-linked firms sounded fairly sanguine about the macro landscape (including earnings from DAL and LEVI , and remarks from WMT’s CFO), and this was in reference to a time before the ceasefire took effect . However, since the SPX was struggling with 6900-7000 even before the war , that range is going to be even stronger resistance now . Meanwhile, the inability of Treasuries to rally Wed (and so far Thurs) is a big red flag , and points to the risk of the war’s inflationary effects being larger and more durable than anticipated (the Mar CPI on Fri is expected to show a huge acceleration vs. Feb).
• What we’ll be watching on Thursday – the only major event on the calendar is the US PCE for Feb at 8:30am ET (the Street is modeling headline unchanged vs. Jan at +2.8% Y/Y while core cools 10bp to +3%).
• What we’ll be watching on Friday – the major events will be the US CPI for Mar at 8:30am ET (the Street is looking for a sharp acceleration in headline to +3.4% vs. +2.4% in Feb while core climbs to +2.7% vs. +2.5% in Feb) and the first meeting of US and Iranian officials in Pakistan (which is set to commence Sat morning Pakistan time).
•
vital.market_in_a_minute_macro:
Market in a Minute (macro) – US/Iran: 8 reasons why the ceasefire is likely to hold ( Vital Knowledge ); Vance will lead a delegation of American officials to Pakistan for talks w/ Iran this weekend ( Axios ); Israel offers to dial back its activity in Lebanon during the US-Iran negotiations ( Axios ); Israel is preparing for a permanent war state despite the efforts by Washington and Tehran to strike a détente ( Reuters ); White House will likely extend a current waiver of certain Russian/Iran oil sanctions ( Semafor ); Eurozone economy is set to be hit with a “stagflationary shock” according to the EU’s top economic official ( FT ); New Zealand left rates unchanged , as expected, but the accompanying language was hawkish , with the central bank warning that it stood ready to tighten policy forcefully if the Middle East war pushed inflation higher ( WSJ ); NATO and the US : the White House is considering a plan to shift American troops out of countries deemed unhelpful during the recent Iran campaign ( WSJ ); Private credit redemptions exceeded $20 B in Q1 as investors pivoted away from the category ( FT )
•

vital.us_macro_section:
US macro

• The stagflationary pulse sparked by the ~5-week war is coursing through the global economy , and it’s not clear when (or if) this will dissipate (it might not be fully out of the data until the late summer or fall).
• The Fed will probably (at best) be on the sidelines this year when it comes to the Funds Rate , and the pace of asset purchases is set to slide meaningfully after this month (while the BOE, ECB, and BOJ are all likely to hike rates this year).
• The Fed minutes Wed afternoon contained a lot of hawkish commentary about upside inflation risks, and some central bank officials wanted to warn markets of the risk for potential rate hikes . That said, Fed officials are still inclined to cut rates assuming data unfolds as anticipated (“many participants judged that, in time, it would likely become appropriate to lower the target range for the federal funds rate if inflation were to decline in line with their expectations”).
• US fertility rate fell to a fresh record low in 2025 , hitting 53.1 births per 1000 women of childbearing age (down from 53.8 in 2024) ( NYT )

Washington

• White House will press forward with a pro-AI regulatory stance despite rising pushback to the technology ( WSJ )
• Republicans increasingly concerned about the party’s mid-term prospects despite the Iran ceasefire ( Politico )
• Chris Phelan , an advisor at the Minneapolis Fed, is the lead candidate to replace Stephen Miran as chair of the Council of Economic Advisors ( Politico )

Int’l macro

• Chinese auto sales slumped 12.5% Y/Y in Mar, the 6 th straight month of declines ( Reuters )
• Chinese autos : GAC said it would begin assembling vehicles in Mexico in H2, becoming the first Chinese auto OEM to establish a production facility in the country ( Motor Trend )
• Eurozone economy is set to be hit with a “stagflationary shock” according to the EU’s top economic official ( FT )
• German industrial production for Feb fell short (-0.3% vs. the Street +0.7%) while exports came in ahead of plan (+3.6% vs. the Street +1.3%) (Bloomberg)
• Japanese consumer confidence slumped to 33.3 in Mar (down from 39.7 in Feb and below the consensus forecast of 38.3) (Bloomberg)
• Mexico will dramatically increase domestic gas production as the country looks to reduce its dependence on American supplies ( FT )
• NATO and the US : the White House is considering a plan to shift American troops out of countries deemed unhelpful during the recent Iran campaign ( WSJ )
• Saudi Arabia abandons plans to expand its massive SWF into semiconductor manufacturing ( Semafor )
• New Zealand left rates unchanged , as expected, but the accompanying language was hawkish , with the central bank warning that it stood ready to tighten policy forcefully if the Middle East war pushed inflation higher ( WSJ )

Consumer

• COST (Costco) reported a modest uptick in core US comps in Mar (+6.2% vs. +6% in Feb) while Canada and Other Int’l both cooled ( Canada came in at +5.4% vs. +9.3% in Feb and Other Int’l was +6.6% vs. +10.9% in Feb).
• Fast Retailing boosted its FY profit/sales forecast following strong H1 results ( Nikkei )
• Indiana became the second state (after Georgia) to suspend its gas tax as governors look to provide relief for their consumers ( Reuters )
• PSMT (Price Smart) reported solid upside in FQ2 on EPS ($1.62 vs. the Street $1.57), EBITDA ($99.7 MM vs. the Street $94 MM), and sales (+9.7% to $1.495 B vs. the Street $1.475 B).
• Seven & I said it would delay an IPO of its North America business ( Reuters )
• STZ (Constellation Brands) reported solid upside in FQ4 on EPS (1.90 vs. the Street 1.73), w/the beat driven by higher sales ($1.92 B vs. the Street $1.87 B, w/ upside in both Beer and esp. Wines & Spirits ) and better margins (the margin declines weren’t as bad as feared in Beer or Wines & Spirits), but the F27 guidance is underwhelming (the EPS mid-point is $11.55 vs. the Street $12.44) due to persistent headwinds (“while we are encouraged by the momentum displayed during the fourth quarter across our Beer and Wine & Spirits businesses, we expect the operating environment to remain dynamic given the evolving socioeconomic backdrop and limited near-term visibility ”).
• WMT (Walmart)’s CFO spoke at a JPMorgan event on Wed and sounded relatively sanguine about the macro landscape despite all the macro uncertainty (“I am probably more constructive on the consumer than what one would glean from reading the headlines of news publications. There's a lot of alarmist headlines and what the impact can be on the consumer. But the consumer continues to be very resilient ”)
Financials

• BLK (Black Rock) shares have performed very well of late as investors focus on its traditional asset mgmt. business instead of the recent private credit-related acquisitions ( WSJ )
• Private credit redemptions exceeded $20 B in Q1 as investors pivoted away from the category ( FT )
M&A/Strategic Actions

• Open AI’s CFO said the company will dedicate a chunk of its upcoming IPO for retail investors ( CNBC )
• Seven & I said it would delay an IPO of its North America business ( Reuters )
TMT

• Anthropic: an appeals court declined to block the Pentagon from blacklisting Anthropic ( Reuters )
• APLD (Applied Digital) reported upside in FQ3 on sales (+139% to $126.6 MM vs. the Street $76.5 MM) and EBITDA ($44.1 MM vs. the Street $20.7 MM), w/ mgmt. very bullish on the macro demand backdrop (“We are seeing a clear acceleration in demand for high-performance AI data center capacity, with hyperscalers as aggressive as we have ever seen them . Just three months ago, we referenced approximately $400 B in annual capital expenditures from the largest U.S. hyperscalers. That figure has now been reported to have increased to nearly $700 B), although free cash flow was ugly due to extremely elevated capex (op. cash flow was $55 MM but purchases of property & equipment was $775 MM, producing free cash flow of -$720 MM vs. the Street -$120 MM ).
• ARM (Arm)’s CEO is expected to take on the additional role of running much of Softbank’s int’l business ( FT )
• DIS (Disney) is planning to cut as many as 1 K jobs in the coming weeks ( WSJ )
• META (Meta) shares spiked on Wed after the company released the first AI model (dubbed Muse Spark) since its recent aggressive hiring/spending spree. Following a NYT report back in Mar about how Meta’s new model was underperforming on internal tests , investors were happy to see Muse Spark formally unveiled , and that it seemed to do decently on benchmarks vs. peers (according to the WSJ , Meta claimed Muse Spark significantly outperformed Grok and did better than Gemini on certain tests while being competitive against Chat GPT and Claude on others ). All that said, there remain a lot of questions about why Meta is pouring so much money into AI given that it has fairly little to show for it (Meta’s various AI initiatives and products lag far behind other frontier firms like Anthropic, Open AI, and Google in terms of market and mindshare , and Muse Spark might, at best, be at parity with those other firms technically , all of whom are rushing to release updated products). It’s a testament to the strength of Meta’s core business that mgmt. can expend so many resources on AI without having to constantly raise fresh capital (a luxury x AI, Open AI, and Anthropic don’t have), but that still doesn’t mean it’s a good idea from an ROI perspective (so far, Apple’s approach to AI, whereby it’s expending minimal cash to repurpose Gemini and earning fees via the App Store on the whole industry, seems like the wisest from a free cash flow perspective).

energy, insurance, and chemicals outperform . Stocks in Asia mostly saw losses on Thursday (with some exceptions): TPX -0.9%, NKY -0.73%, Hang Seng -0.54%, HSCEI -0.75%, SHCOMP -0.72%, Shenzhen -0.61%, Taiwan TAIEX +0.29%, Korea KOSPI -1.61%, Australia ASX 200 +0.24%, and India is down ~1.1-1.3%. Treasuries are flat across the curve, and Fed expectations aren’t moving much either (the market is pricing in 7bp of rate cuts this year). The DXY is ticking down 10bp. Brent is rallying 340bp to ~$98 (the recent low was ~$90.40 from Wed morning). Gold is rallying 30bp while silver trades flat. Bitcoin is about unchanged at ~$71.3 K.
• What’s happening this morning – equities are giving back a bit of the Wed rally as investors sit on the edge of their seats to see whether the fragile ceasefire holds . Incremental news since the US close yesterday has been pretty quiet , and the next major (scheduled) event will be the (tentative) US-Iran meeting set to take place Sat morning in Pakistan (late on Friday in the US). While there were ceasefire violations earlier Wed, it seems like the détente is very much in place so far Thurs – there were no reports of strikes on Iran, and Iran seems to have fired off no drones/missiles of its own , while the foreign ministers of Iran and Saudi Arabia spoke this morning for the first time since the war commenced . The two big flies in the ceasefire ointment are Lebanon (Israel apparently promised Washington it would dial back the intensity of its strikes against Hezbollah for the next two weeks, but this theater is not included in the ceasefire) and Hormuz (where traffic remains depressed and Iran seems to be pushing forward with plans for a toll). In AI , the big news isn’t Meta’s Muse Spark (which we wrote about here on Wed) but instead the new models from Anthropic and Open AI . Anthropic’s warnings about its new model being so powerful that it couldn’t be released right away to the public weighed heavily on software sentiment , and it sounds like Open AI will be adopting a similar approach to its latest model (according to Axios ).
• How we’re thinking about markets – there is a lot of skepticism and cynicism when it comes to the cease fire and this, coupled with continued technical imbalances (in terms of lean long positioning and negative sentiment) point to more equity upside in the immediate term (we think the ceasefire will hold , for reasons outlined in a piece published Wed morning; the next major event will probably be the face-to-face talks between US and Iranian officials tentatively scheduled for Friday in Pakistan ). In addition, consumer-linked firms sounded fairly sanguine about the macro landscape (including earnings from DAL and LEVI , and remarks from WMT’s CFO), and this was in reference to a time before the ceasefire took effect . However, since the SPX was struggling with 6900-7000 even before the war , that range is going to be even stronger resistance now . Meanwhile, the inability of Treasuries to rally Wed (and so far Thurs) is a big red flag , and points to the risk of the war’s inflationary effects being larger and more durable than anticipated (the Mar CPI on Fri is expected to show a huge acceleration vs. Feb).
• What we’ll be watching on Thursday – the only major event on the calendar is the US PCE for Feb at 8:30am ET (the Street is modeling headline unchanged vs. Jan at +2.8% Y/Y while core cools 10bp to +3%).
• What we’ll be watching on Friday – the major events will be the US CPI for Mar at 8:30am ET (the Street is looking for a sharp acceleration in headline to +3.4% vs. +2.4% in Feb while core climbs to +2.7% vs. +2.5% in Feb) and the first meeting of US and Iranian officials in Pakistan (which is set to commence Sat morning Pakistan time).

• Market in a Minute (micro – Friday morning) – APLD (Applied Digital) reported upside in FQ3 on sales (+139% to $126.6 MM vs. the Street $76.5 MM) and EBITDA ($44.1 MM vs. the Street $20.7 MM), w/mgmt. very bullish on the macro demand backdrop, although free cash flow was ugly due to extremely elevated capex (op. cash flow was $55 MM but purchases of property & equipment was $775 MM, producing free cash flow of -$720 MM vs. the Street -$120 MM); DIS (Disney) is planning to cut as many as 1 K jobs in the coming weeks ( WSJ ); STZ (Constellation Brands) reported solid upside in FQ4 on EPS (1.90 vs. the Street 1.73), w/the beat driven by higher sales ($1.92 B vs. the Street $1.87 B, w/upside in both Beer and esp. Wines & Spirits) and better margins (the margin declines weren’t as bad as feared in Beer or Wines & Spirits), but the F27 guidance is underwhelming (the EPS mid-point is $11.55 vs. the Street $12.44) due to persistent headwinds.
• Calendar for Thursday 4/9 – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( SMPL ), US personal income/spending for Feb (8:30am ET), the US PCE for Feb (8:30am ET), the US GDP revisions for Q4 (8:30am ET ), US wholesale inventories/trade sales for Feb (10am ET), earnings after the US close ( WDFC ), Japan’s PPI for Mar (Thurs night), China’s PPI/CPI for Mar (Thurs night), and the Bank of Korea policy decision (Thurs night).
• Calendar for Friday 4/10 – the focus will be on Taiwan’s imports/exports for Mar (4am ET), the US-Iran meeting in Pakistan , the US CPI for Mar (8:30am ET), US factory orders for Feb (10am ET), and the Michigan sentiment survey for Apr (10am ET).

For US earnings , the key reports include: Mon morning ( GS ); Tues morning ( BLK, C, JNJ, JPM, KMX , and WFC ); Wed morning ( BAC, PNC , and MS ); Wed night ( SLG ); Thurs morning ( ABT, BK, CFG, KEY, MRSH, PEP, SCHW, TRV , and USB ); Thurs night ( AA and NFLX ); and Fri morning ( RF, STT , and TFC ). In Europe, the key earnings reports include: Tues night ( Kering ); Wed morning ( ASML and Hermes ); Thurs morning ( Entain and Pernod Ricard ); and Fri morning ( Ericsson ).
Iran

• Despite obstacles and friction on Wed to the ceasefire (Tehran accused the ceasefire of being violated while countries in the Middle East were forced to defend themselves from Iranian drones and missiles, and the war in Lebanon continues to rage), and ongoing questions about Hormuz , both the US and Iran seem to be pushing forward with diplomacy for the time being , with a face-to-face meeting set to take place in Pakistan this weekend .
• US/Iran: 8 reasons why the ceasefire is likely to hold ( Vital Knowledge )
• Trump said US military assets will stay in the Middle East and warned that fighting will recommence if a deal w/Iran isn’t reached (Trump added that he anticipates an agreement will ultimately be struck ) (Truth Social)
• Vance will lead a delegation of American officials to Pakistan for talks w/ Iran this weekend ( Axios )
• White House warned against declaring victory prematurely in the Middle East – Iran’s regime remains firmly entrenched , it still possesses powerful missile capabilities , and its control over Hormuz is as strong as ever ( WSJ )
• The Middle East won’t just go back to the way it was before . The market will embed a significant risk premium on all commodities traveling through Hormuz for the foreseeable future, especially since Iran still seems to have full control over the waterway (according to the FT , Iran will charge a $1/barrel “tolling fee” , payable in bitcoin , for all tankers moving through Hormuz).
• Israel offers to dial back its activity in Lebanon during the US-Iran negotiations ( Axios )
• Israel is preparing for a permanent war state despite the efforts by Washington and Tehran to strike a détente ( Reuters )
• White House will likely extend a current waiver of certain Russian/Iran oil sanctions ( Semafor )
• US oil exports are set to hit a record high in April as demand climbs due to Middle East disruptions ( FT )
• Oil industry execs urge the White House to block Iran’s proposed Hormuz toll ( Politico )
US macro

• The stagflationary pulse sparked by the ~5-week war is coursing through the global economy , and it’s not clear when (or if) this will dissipate (it might not be fully out of the data until the late summer or fall).
• The Fed will probably (at best) be on the sidelines this year when it comes to the Funds Rate , and the pace of asset purchases is set to slide meaningfully after this month (while the BOE, ECB, and BOJ are all likely to hike rates this year).
• The Fed minutes Wed afternoon contained a lot of hawkish commentary about upside inflation risks, and some central bank officials wanted to warn markets of the risk for potential rate hikes . That said, Fed officials are still inclined to cut rates assuming data unfolds as anticipated (“many participants judged that, in time, it would likely become appropriate to lower the target range for the federal funds rate if inflation were to decline in line with their expectations”).
• US fertility rate fell to a fresh record low in 2025 , hitting 53.1 births per 1000 women of childbearing age (down from 53.8 in 2024) ( NYT )

Washington

• White House will press forward with a pro-AI regulatory stance despite rising pushback to the technology ( WSJ )
• Republicans increasingly concerned about the party’s mid-term prospects despite the Iran ceasefire ( Politico )
• Chris Phelan , an advisor at the Minneapolis Fed, is the lead candidate to replace Stephen Miran as chair of the Council of Economic Advisors ( Politico )

Int’l macro

• Chinese auto sales slumped 12.5% Y/Y in Mar, the 6 th straight month of declines ( Reuters )
• Chinese autos : GAC said it would begin assembling vehicles in Mexico in H2, becoming the first Chinese auto OEM to establish a production facility in the country ( Motor Trend )
• Eurozone economy is set to be hit with a “stagflationary shock” according to the EU’s top economic official ( FT )
• German industrial production for Feb fell short (-0.3% vs. the Street +0.7%) while exports came in ahead of plan (+3.6% vs. the Street +1.3%) (Bloomberg)
• Japanese consumer confidence slumped to 33.3 in Mar (down from 39.7 in Feb and below the consensus forecast of 38.3) (Bloomberg)
• Mexico will dramatically increase domestic gas production as the country looks to reduce its dependence on American supplies ( FT )
• NATO and the US : the White House is considering a plan to shift American troops out of countries deemed unhelpful during the recent Iran campaign ( WSJ )
• Saudi Arabia abandons plans to expand its massive SWF into semiconductor manufacturing ( Semafor )
• New Zealand left rates unchanged , as expected, but the accompanying language was hawkish , with the central bank warning that it stood ready to tighten policy forcefully if the Middle East war pushed inflation higher ( WSJ )

Consumer

• COST (Costco) reported a modest uptick in core US comps in Mar (+6.2% vs. +6% in Feb) while Canada and Other Int’l both cooled ( Canada came in at +5.4% vs. +9.3% in Feb and Other Int’l was +6.6% vs. +10.9% in Feb).
• Fast Retailing boosted its FY profit/sales forecast following strong H1 results ( Nikkei )
• Indiana became the second state (after Georgia) to suspend its gas tax as governors look to provide relief for their consumers ( Reuters )
• PSMT (Price Smart) reported solid upside in FQ2 on EPS ($1.62 vs. the Street $1.57), EBITDA ($99.7 MM vs. the Street $94 MM), and sales (+9.7% to $1.495 B vs. the Street $1.475 B).
• Seven & I said it would delay an IPO of its North America business ( Reuters )
• STZ (Constellation Brands) reported solid upside in FQ4 on EPS (1.90 vs. the Street 1.73), w/the beat driven by higher sales ($1.92 B vs. the Street $1.87 B, w/ upside in both Beer and esp. Wines & Spirits ) and better margins (the margin declines weren’t as bad as feared in Beer or Wines & Spirits), but the F27 guidance is underwhelming (the EPS mid-point is $11.55 vs. the Street $12.44) due to persistent headwinds (“while we are encouraged by the momentum displayed during the fourth quarter across our Beer and Wine & Spirits businesses, we expect the operating environment to remain dynamic given the evolving socioeconomic backdrop and limited near-term visibility ”).
• WMT (Walmart)’s CFO spoke at a JPMorgan event on Wed and sounded relatively sanguine about the macro landscape despite all the macro uncertainty (“I am probably more constructive on the consumer than what one would glean from reading the headlines of news publications. There's a lot of alarmist headlines and what the impact can be on the consumer. But the consumer continues to be very resilient ”)
vital.tmt_section:
TMT

• Anthropic: an appeals court declined to block the Pentagon from blacklisting Anthropic ( Reuters )
• APLD (Applied Digital) reported upside in FQ3 on sales (+139% to $126.6 MM vs. the Street $76.5 MM) and EBITDA ($44.1 MM vs. the Street $20.7 MM), w/ mgmt. very bullish on the macro demand backdrop (“We are seeing a clear acceleration in demand for high-performance AI data center capacity, with hyperscalers as aggressive as we have ever seen them . Just three months ago, we referenced approximately $400 B in annual capital expenditures from the largest U.S. hyperscalers. That figure has now been reported to have increased to nearly $700 B), although free cash flow was ugly due to extremely elevated capex (op. cash flow was $55 MM but purchases of property & equipment was $775 MM, producing free cash flow of -$720 MM vs. the Street -$120 MM ).
• ARM (Arm)’s CEO is expected to take on the additional role of running much of Softbank’s int’l business ( FT )
• DIS (Disney) is planning to cut as many as 1 K jobs in the coming weeks ( WSJ )
• META (Meta) shares spiked on Wed after the company released the first AI model (dubbed Muse Spark) since its recent aggressive hiring/spending spree. Following a NYT report back in Mar about how Meta’s new model was underperforming on internal tests , investors were happy to see Muse Spark formally unveiled , and that it seemed to do decently on benchmarks vs. peers (according to the WSJ , Meta claimed Muse Spark significantly outperformed Grok and did better than Gemini on certain tests while being competitive against Chat GPT and Claude on others ). All that said, there remain a lot of questions about why Meta is pouring so much money into AI given that it has fairly little to show for it (Meta’s various AI initiatives and products lag far behind other frontier firms like Anthropic, Open AI, and Google in terms of market and mindshare , and Muse Spark might, at best, be at parity with those other firms technically , all of whom are rushing to release updated products). It’s a testament to the strength of Meta’s core business that mgmt. can expend so many resources on AI without having to constantly raise fresh capital (a luxury x AI, Open AI, and Anthropic don’t have), but that still doesn’t mean it’s a good idea from an ROI perspective (so far, Apple’s approach to AI, whereby it’s expending minimal cash to repurpose Gemini and earning fees via the App Store on the whole industry, seems like the wisest from a free cash flow perspective).
• Open AI will copy Anthropic and release its new model in a staggered fashion given powerful capabilities that increase cybersecurity risks ( Axios )
• Open AI’s CFO said the company will dedicate a chunk of its upcoming IPO for retail investors ( CNBC )
• Saudi Arabia abandons plans to expand its massive SWF into semiconductor manufacturing ( Semafor )
• TTWO (Take Two)’s GTA 6 may finally see the light of day as the Nov 19 launch date is unlikely to be pushed back further ( Seeking Alpha )
• White House will press forward with a pro-AI regulatory stance despite rising pushback to the technology ( WSJ )

reuters.body_excerpt:
Plus, Tesla is developing a new smaller, cheaper EV.

Daily Briefing

Daily Briefing

By Kate Turton

Hello. Israel's bombing of Lebanon puts the Middle East ceasefire in further jeopardy , and Iran emerges from the war with a powerful chokehold on the Strait of Hormuz.

Elsewhere, Tesla is developing a new smaller, cheaper EV , and patients searching for estrogen patches report pharmacy-hopping and dose changes.

Today's Top News

A man walks at the site of an Israeli strike, in Al-Mazraa in Beirut, Lebanon. REUTERS/Yara Nard

War in the Middle East

•

Israel bombed more targets in Lebanon, putting the Middle East ceasefire in further jeopardy after its biggest attacks of the war on its neighbor killed more than 250 people and threatened to torpedo Donald Trump's truce from the outset .

• Hundreds were killed in Lebanon yesterday during the most intense Israeli strikes since its current campaign against Hezbollah began. Reuters Bureau Chief for Lebanon Maya Gebeily tells the Reuters World News podcast why people across the country are worried.
•

Nearly six weeks of war in Iran have ended, for now, with Trump claiming victory, but the US-Iran ceasefire locks in a harsh reality: an entrenched, radical government with control over the Strait of Hormuz and a powerful lever over global energy markets and Gulf rivals, analysts say.
•

Investors placed an approximately $950 million bet on oil prices falling just hours before the US and Iran announced a ceasefire , the latest large wager on the direction of the world's most traded commodity ahead of a major policy announcement by President Donald Trump.

In other news

•

A lobbying firm led by one of Donald Trump Jr.'s hunting buddies helped a Chinese company make its case to a US national security watchdog against a US startup that was seeking to have the Chinese firm removed as one of its investors, public filings show.
•

A US Army veteran was charged with providing classified information to a journalist for a book that alleged drug trafficking, murder ‌and corruption at a military base where she had worked, the Department of Justice said.
•

Britain's defense minister ‌John Healey said the UK ⁠had tracked Russian submarines in the north Atlantic for a month, ‌which ⁠he said was a threat to ⁠British cables and pipelines.
•
Pope Leo's upcoming visit to four countries in Africa will be an ambitious tour to urge global leaders to address the needs of the continent where more than a fifth of the world's Catholics live on his first major overseas trip of ‌2026.

Business & Markets

•

Share markets sagged as cracks quickly began to appear in the fragile Gulf truce, nudging oil prices back up toward $100 a barrel and reminding investors the inflationary fallout would last a long while yet . And the Iran war doubles Russia's main oil revenue to $9 billion. Read our exclusive.

• A Washington, D.C., federal appeals court declined to block the Pentagon's national security blacklisting of AI ‌company Anthropic for now, a win for the Trump administration that comes after another appeals court came to the opposite conclusion in a separate legal challenge by Anthropic.
•
Tesla is developing an all-new smaller, cheaper electric SUV, four people familiar with the matter told Reuters. The automaker has contacted suppliers in recent weeks to discuss details of the plan for the compact SUV. For more on the EV revolution and sustainable business, sign up for Ross Kerber's Sustainable Finance Newsletter .

• Car exports, an increasingly important source of growth for China's hyper-competitive auto sector , picked up pace in March despite shipment disruptions from the crisis in ‌the Middle East, one of the industry's key overseas markets.
•

A surge in demand since last year for estrogen patches to ease menopause symptoms has strained supplies and led to shortages, sparking a scramble for medicines that industry sources say could last up to three years.
•

Whether it’s Kraft Heinz weighing a split or Unilever sealing a $65 billion mayonnaise deal, edible empires are coming apart. In this week’s Viewsroom podcast , Breakingviews columnists debate consumer groups’ sticky spot.

Trump’s mixed messages and Iran’s bombs kept the Kurds out of the war

A female fighter from from the Kurdistan Freedom Party, known as PAK, carries a weapon and gestures while training near Erbil, Iraq, in mid-February. REUTERS/Stringer

Iran’s Revolutionary Guards deterred the Kurds from rising up through a combination of military strikes in neighboring Iraq and threats against Kurds on both sides of the border. Ambiguous messages from the US and Israel also left the Kurds confused about their role.

Read our special report

Is this the toughest hole in golf?

Northern Ireland's Rory Mc Ilroy plays out from the bunker on the 12th hole during a practice round REUTERS/Eloisa Lopez

Considered the toughest hole in golf by none other than 18-times major winner Jack Nicklaus, the 12th hole at Augusta National

filtered_signal.driver_principal: The fragile Middle East ceasefire between the US and Iran is the dominant factor influencing market risk perception, driving elevated oil prices and inflation concerns.
hechos_confirmados: A tentative two-week ceasefire between the US and Iran is in place, with a face-to-face meeting scheduled in Pakistan for April 10-11, 2026.
Israel has continued bombing targets in Lebanon, which is not included in the ceasefire, putting the truce at risk.
Iran controls access to the Strait of Hormuz and is pushing plans to charge a toll for tanker traffic, contributing to elevated oil prices above $100 per barrel.
US equity futures are down, and Treasury yields are steady, reflecting market caution amid geopolitical uncertainty and inflation concerns.
filtered_signal.escenario_dominante: There is a moderate to high probability that the ceasefire between the US and Iran will hold through the upcoming talks, supporting some near-term equity upside. However, ongoing regional tensions, especially Israeli strikes in Lebanon and Iran's control over Hormuz, will sustain elevated oil prices and inflationary pressures, limiting Treasury rallies and maintaining market volatility.
filtered_signal.interpretacion_institucional: The market is cautiously optimistic about the ceasefire holding, as reflected in recent equity gains, but skepticism remains due to violations and unresolved conflicts in Lebanon and Hormuz. Elevated oil prices signal persistent supply risks, embedding a higher inflation premium that may challenge central banks' easing expectations. The upcoming US-Iran talks are critical for confirming the ceasefire's durability. Investors should monitor inflation data closely, as a potential acceleration could prompt a more hawkish Fed stance, while geopolitical developments will continue to influence risk sentiment and commodity markets.
riesgos_secundarios: The ceasefire could collapse if Israeli strikes in Lebanon escalate or if Iran increases military activity, reigniting broader conflict.
Iran's enforcement of a toll on the Strait of Hormuz could further disrupt global energy supply and exacerbate inflation.
US and allied military repositioning or policy shifts could alter regional dynamics and market risk appetite.
Inflation may accelerate more than expected, forcing central banks to tighten monetary policy despite geopolitical risks.
Market sentiment could deteriorate if technical imbalances and negative positioning intensify, leading to sharper equity corrections.
